coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional carbon quantum dot-embedded metal-organic framework nanohybrid as a highly efficient electrocatalyst for water splitting and CO2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Hedau, Bhavna
Park, Sang-Joon
Kang, Byeong-Cheol
Ha, Tae-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Bifunctional carbon quantum dot (CQD)-embedded metal-organic framework (MOF) (CQD@MOF) nanohybrid as an energy-efficient electrocatalyst is fabricated for electrochemical water splitting and CO2 reduction through a simple and environmentally friendly method using natural resources. Key parameters, such as synthesis temperature, deposition temperature and time, pH and type of electrolyte, and scan rate, are optimized to fabricate binder-free CQD@MOF/nickel foam (NF) electrodes. Excellent hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions occur with a current density of 50 mA/cm2 at lower overpotentials of 48.00 and 135 mV in a 1 M potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte at a scan rate of 5 mV/s, respectively. In a two-electrode system, the CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibits a low overpotential of 1.36 V to achieve 10 mA/cm2, confirming the practical feasibility of overall water splitting. Furthermore, the electrode exhibits high chemical stability and robustness even after 4000 cycles and more than 17 h. These results are attributed to the effective combination of substantial adsorption sites and the improved conductivity of the MOF induced by the electron-accepting characteristics of the CQDs with high charge transfer rates during water splitting. Finally, electrochemical CO2 reduction of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode is demonstrated by continuously maintaining a specific current density for 12 h, which can extend the technological potential of carbon nanohybrid as an electrocatalyst for green energy applications.

(a) Schematic of the synthesis process of CQD@MOF nanohybrid. Morphological characteristics of (b) the MOF and (c) CQD@MOF obtained via SEM, and (d) CQD@MOF obtained via TEM. (e) High-resolution TEM images of CQD@MOF. (f) EDS elemental mapping profile of CQD@MOF: HAADF-STEM image, C, O, and Zr. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Material characterization of the CQD, MOF, and CQD@MOF. Deconvoluted XPS comparative spectra of (a) C1s, (b) O1s, and (c) Zr3d. (d) FTIR spectra, e) XRD patterns, and (e) BJH pore size distribution of the MOF and CQD@MOF. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic of the mechanism for the improved (a) HER and (b) OER of the CQD@MOF proposed in this study. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Double-layer capacitance for the (i) HER and (j) OER, and Nyquist plot for the (k) HER and (l) OER. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

LSV polarization curves of the CQD@ MOF/NF electrode in different electrolytes with different pH (0.5 M H2SO4 (pH = 1), 1 M PBS (pH = 7), and 1 M KOH (pH = 14)) for the (b) HER and (c) OER, and different molar concentrations of the KOH solution (1, 3, and 6 M) for the (d) HER and (e) OER. (f) HER polarization curves of the CQD@MOF in water and 1 M KOH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Long-term stability and durability tests of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode in 1 M KOH. (a) HER and (b) OER polarization curves during the initial CV and after 3000 CV cycles. Chronoamperometric durability tests for the (c) HER and (d) OER. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

OWS performance of the developed CQD@MOF/NF electrode. (a) Optical image of a two-electrode system for OWS in which the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode acts as both the anode and cathode. (b) Comparative OWS polarization curves and (c) corresponding Tafel plots of CQD@MOF/NF || CQD@MOF/NF and bare NF || NF in 1 M KOH. (d) LSV polarization curves during the initial CV and after 4000 CV cycles. (e) Long-term stability as a function of time. (f) Comparative overpotential at 10 mA/cm2 for the chronopotentiometry durability test of CQD@MOF/NF kept in ambient air for 60 d. (g) H2 and (h) O2 generation at 0, 5, 10, and 15 min using the customized gas-collection equipment. (i) Volume of H2 and O2 produced from CQD@MOF/NF in a 1 M KOH aqueous solution as a function of time.

CO2 reduction on the NF, MOF/NF, CQD/NF, and CQD@MOF/NF electrodes. (a) LSV polarization curves in 0.1 M NaOH and (b) corresponding Tafel plots. (c) CO Faradaic efficiency, (d) Chronoamperometric durability at a constant voltage of 0.637 V. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of the OER, HER, and OWS performance of the CQD@MOF, as reported in literature.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional carbon quantum dot-embedded metal-organic framework nanohybrid as a highly efficient electrocatalyst for water splitting and CO2 reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept. of Electronic Materials Engineering, Kwangwoon University, Seoul, 01897, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dept. of Electronic Materials Engineering, Kwangwoon University, Seoul, 01897, South Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bifunctional carbon quantum dot (CQD)-embedded metal-organic framework (MOF) (CQD@MOF) nanohybrid as an energy-efficient electrocatalyst is fabricated for electrochemical water splitting and CO2 reduction through a simple and environmentally friendly method using natural resources. Key parameters, such as synthesis temperature, deposition temperature and time, pH and type of electrolyte, and scan rate, are optimized to fabricate binder-free CQD@MOF/nickel foam (NF) electrodes. Excellent hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions occur with a current density of 50 mA/cm2 at lower overpotentials of 48.00 and 135 mV in a 1 M potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte at a scan rate of 5 mV/s, respectively. In a two-electrode system, the CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibits a low overpotential of 1.36 V to achieve 10 mA/cm2, confirming the practical feasibility of overall water splitting. Furthermore, the electrode exhibits high chemical stability and robustness even after 4000 cycles and more than 17 h. These results are attributed to the effective combination of substantial adsorption sites and the improved conductivity of the MOF induced by the electron-accepting characteristics of the CQDs with high charge transfer rates during water splitting. Finally, electrochemical CO2 reduction of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode is demonstrated by continuously maintaining a specific current density for 12 h, which can extend the technological potential of carbon nanohybrid as an electrocatalyst for green energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrocatalytic water splitting is a promising approach for renewable and clean energy technologies owing to the simplicity of splitting H2O molecules into H2 and O2 [1]. To improve the water-splitting efficiency, significant efforts have been devoted toward the comprehensive investigation of the material design and chemical synthesis of ideal electrocatalysts [2]. Highly efficient electrocatalysts should exhibit high intrinsic electrocatalytic activity and operational stability to overcome the large overpotential required to effectively split water molecules through the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [3]. In addition, they should possess a bifunctional ability that can simultaneously drive the HER and OER at a low overpotential in a two-electrode system, which should be involved for overall water splitting (OWS) in practical energy applications [4]. Conventionally, noble metals, such as Pt and Ir/Ru and their oxides, have been investigated for water splitting because of their near-zero overpotential [5]. Low-cost and high-performance bifunctional non-noble metal-based electrocatalysts have been extensively explored as alternatives to noble metals for practical water splitting applications because of the high scarcity and cost of noble metals [6,7].

Metal-organic framework (MOF) has been considered a promising alternative to conventional electrocatalysts owing to their tunable composition and structural integration [8]. With abundant pore structures and active sites for H+/OH− adsorption for the HER and OER, MOFs have demonstrated excellent potential in water electrocatalysis [9]. However, pristine MOF as a bifunctional electrocatalyst has the disadvantages of inherent low conductivity and poor chemical stability, especially in the OER [10]. To overcome these intrinsic limitations, various strategies, including heterometal doping, defect engineering, morphology tuning, heterostructure construction, and hybridization of alloys with conductive functional materials, have been extensively investigated [11–13]. The integration of MOFs with advanced functional materials has garnered considerable attention owing to the improved electrical conductivity and chemical stability [12–15]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a promising candidate for rapid electrochemical reactions owing to their excellent biocompatibility, high chemical stability, low fabrication cost, high electrical conductivity, and surface-tunable functionality [16,17]. As electron acceptors, CQDs can substantially improve the electron transfer efficiency and electrocatalytic activity of their parent materials [18,19]. Therefore, such electrocatalytic activity can be induced by the synergistic action of the numerous active metal sites of porous MOF and the high electrical conductivity of CQDs.

The application of MOF-based nanohybrids in energy systems is impeded by their non-biodegradability, complex separation techniques, and undesirable environmental implications for water splitting, which are attributed to the requirement of water-insoluble toxic solvents, non-renewable feedstocks, and the formation of hazardous end-products [20]. For example, highly toxic solvents, such as N, N-dimethylmethanamide, used during MOF synthesis and the insolubility of the prepared MOFs can severely affect human health and the environment [21]. To suppress the toxicity of MOFs, toxic solvents are replaced with less harmful ones or are diluted in deionized (DI) water [21]. However, this approach can degrade the electrocatalytic quality, catalytic activity, and production yield and inhibit surface modification with functional groups and structural formation, which hinder the practical application of MOF in water electrolysis [22]. Therefore, biocompatible and eco-friendly methods for the synthesis of advanced electrocatalysts, such as the green synthesis of CQDs obtained directly from natural sources containing citric acid, have been intensively investigated [23]. Furthermore, an intermediate solution for reducing toxicity while maintaining high catalytic efficiency is crucial for green energy applications [24]. Considering the current demand, issues associated with water splitting, such as environmental risks, fabrication costs, toxic processes, and inefficient bifunctional catalysts, must be addressed through the green synthesis of ideal electrocatalysts based on natural sources [24].

Herein, we demonstrate a CQD-embedded MOF (CQD@MOF) as a rapid and highly efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for OWS and CO2 reduction, fabricated using a hydrothermal method. The synergetic effect of MOF and CQD is investigated to realize a high-performance bifunctional electrocatalyst. We note that very few studies have focused on CQDs directly extracted from kiwi, as a natural source, for highly efficient water splitting. To decrease the environmental impact of the MOF synthesis, in this study, the dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent is replaced with DI water. Next, we investigate binder-free CQD@MOF/nickel foam (NF) as the working electrode for water splitting and analyze its performance toward the HER and OER, including the efficiency, stability, and durability of the OWS cell. The synthesis mechanism for the formation of MOF crystals guided by the functional groups on the CQD surface is investigated for the development of the CQD@MOF nanohybrids. Finally, we demonstrate the catalytic activity toward CO2 reduction using the prepared CQD@MOF/NF electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characteristics of MOF, CQDs, and CQD@MOF
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method towards top-down route using kiwi as the carbon source. Typically, citric acid is used as a source of sp2 hybridized carbon networks [25,26]. Kiwi contains 40–60 % citric acid and 10 % malic acid, which allows us to use it as a natural precursor of CQD [25–27]. For extracting optimal results, CQDs were hydrothermally obtained at varying temperatures (100, 140, 180, and 220 °C) for 5 h, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The CQD size varies with the synthesis temperature , which prompts its nucleation under different conditions [28]. Increasing the temperature during the hydrothermal process can effectively carbonize the natural precursor into CQDs [24]. Moreover, the increased CQD size can broaden the occupied and unoccupied orbitals, thereby decreasing the band-gaps [24,29]. The synthesis temperature was optimized to obtain improved and large CQDs prior to further application. To investigate the relative change in the CQD size with increasing temperature, fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) measurements were performed, where the change in the CQD size is directly related to the difference in the Stokes shift [30–32]. Fig. 1b–e shows the FL emissions of the CQDs obtained at different synthesis temperatures (100, 140, 180, and 220 °C). The emission spectra obtained provided information on the emission of each sample at the excitation wavelengths of 320–480 nm. The CQD aqueous solution emitted blue FL (Fig. 1a) under a 365 nm ultraviolet light and was completely transparent under daylight. The intensity of the emission spectra increased with the temperature, as shown in Fig. 1b–d. Consequently, the Stokes shifts exhibited a similar trend. However, an opposite trend was noted at temperatures exceeding 180 °C. In particular, the intensity of the emission peaks at 220 °C was reduced (Fig. 1e). To compare the emission of CQDs synthesized at different temperatures, we compared FL spectra excited at 360 nm wavelength (Fig. S1). The highest FL intensity was obtained from CQDs synthesized at 180 °C. As shown in Fig. 1a, the CQDs synthesized at 180 °C illuminated the brightest blue light, compared to others, which is in good agreement with the analysis of FL intensity. These results indicate that at temperatures higher than 180 °C, the precursor degraded before CQD formation. Furthermore, the CQDs synthesized at 180 °C exhibited the largest Stokes shift, which was ascribed to the effect of the functional groups at higher temperatures, whereas a smaller Stokes shift was observed for the CQDs synthesized at 220 °C, which reduced the functional groups when the optimum temperature was exceeded [30]. As shown in Fig. S2a-e, 5 samples of CQD synthesized in different batches at different times exhibited similar fluorescence spectra excited at 320 nm–480 nm range. These results indicate that our protocol for CQD preparation enables reproduction of the synthesis. The CQD@MOF nanohybrid was obtained via a hydrothermal method with a Zr cluster as the central metal source and biphenyl-4,4-dicarboxylic acid (H2bpdc) as the linker group at 120 °C for 20 h (Fig. 2 a). The surface morphologies of the prepared samples were observed through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2b–i and S3). The pristine MOF exhibited a nanoflower-like structure with an average diameter of 1 μm, as shown in Fig. 2b and S2a, which is similar to that reported in the literature [33]. In addition, the SEM image of the CQD@MOF nanohybrid (Fig. 2c and S3b) exhibited a similar nanoflower-like structure with an average diameter of 1.2 μm, which was slightly larger than that of the pristine MOF [33,34]. These results indicate that the incorporation of CQDs did not destroy the crystal structure of the Zr-based framework. Fig. S3b shows the reconstruction of nanoflowers using multiple interpenetrated ultrathin nanosheets with smooth surfaces. The pristine MOF exhibited an incomplete petal structure, in relation to the CQD@MOF nanohybrid with a completely grown flower-like structure [34,35]. The presence of CQDs in the CQD@MOF nanohybrid was verified through TEM, as shown in Fig. 2d and e. The CQD diameters were 2–6 nm, as confirmed by the crystal matrix/petal-like structure of the CQD@MOF nanohybrid (Fig. S3c), which indicates that the CQDs were well-incorporated into the MOF matrix. As shown in Fig. 2e, the specific darker grey zone from widely dispersed spherical morphology has a diameter of ∼6 nm and lattice fringes with an inter-planar space of 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the facet of graphitic carbon and is relevant with an average lattice spacing found in CQDs (0.20–0.30 nm) [36]. Based on the spherical structure with diameter and lattice spacing obtained from TEM characterization, the darker grey zones can be determined as CQDs rather than regular atomic layer. Subsequently, the surface elements were analyzed through energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), as shown in Figs. S3d and e. The EDS spectra revealed the presence of C, O, and Zr on the surfaces of the MOF and CQD@MOF nanoflowers. The C content increased from 78 % to 91 %, whereas the O and Zr content decreased from 5 % to 3 % and 19 %–8 %, respectively. Furthermore, selected-area electron diffraction and EDS mapping results (Fig. 2f) indicate the uniform distribution of each element in the CQD@MOF nanohybrid.

To verify the surface chemical composition and corresponding interactions among the CQD, MOF, and CQD@MOF, their X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 3a–c and S3a. Two major peaks at 284.8 and 531.5 eV were observed in the survey spectra of the CQD, MOF, and CQD@MOF, which indicate the presence of C1s and O1s, respectively [36]. Additionally, the third peak at 182.8 eV in the survey spectra of the MOF and the CQD@MOF nanohybrid revealed the existence of Zr3d, as shown in Fig. S4a [36]. The deconvolution of each element is shown in Fig. 3 a–c, where the presence of different states for each element are indicated. The C1s spectra of the CQDs are composed of two peaks associated with C–C (284.8 eV) and C–O (286.4 eV) [37]. The C1s spectra of the MOF and CQD@MOF exhibited two distinct peaks at 284.7 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to the oxygen containing free metal groups C–C and CO, respectively (Fig. 3a), which indicates the availability of sufficient reaction sites for the electrocatalytic process [38]. The peak assigned to CO was absent in the C1s spectra of the CQDs because the C–C bonds were mainly present on the surface, as shown the peak corresponding to the CO bonds in the deconvoluted O1s spectra of the CQDs [38]. The O1s spectra of the CQDs were deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.4 and 532.6 eV, corresponding to the CO and C–OH/C–O–C bonds of the carboxylate group, respectively (Fig. 3b) [38]. In contrast, the two characteristic peaks in the O1s spectra of the MOF and CQD@MOF at 530.0 and 531.6 eV correspond to the Zr–O–Zr and CO bonds, respectively [39]. The ratio of peaks for adsorbed oxygen and lattice oxygen can be used to determine the relative concentrations of oxygen vacancy (V0), which has been widely utilized for the identification of V0 through XPS characterization [40–42]. We utilized the same method to determine V0 from the fitted areas of the O1s spectra using the following equation [40–42]:
(1)

The V0 of CQD@MOF (92 %) was higher than that of the pristine MOF (71 %). The deconvoluted spectra of Zr3d had two consistent peaks for MOF and CQD@MOF, as shown in Fig. 3c. The peaks at 182.9 and 185.2 eV associated with Zr5/2 and Zr3/2, respectively, remained unchanged in the spectra of CQD@MOF, indicating that the central metal ion did not interact with the CQDs [39]. Therefore, the maintained peaks and significant changes in V0 proved the improved catalytic properties of the pristine MOF with the incorporation of CQDs. The XPS spectrum of CQD@MOF was not significantly impacted, compared to that of pristine MOF, which could be attributed to the confinement of CQD in the pores of MOF rather than that on the surface of MOF [40–42]. The existing interactions between the functional groups were analyzed through Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3d. The FTIR spectra of the CQDs exhibited a wide absorption band at 3600 - 3000 cm−1, corresponding to the O–H stretching vibrations [43]. Similarly, the absorption peaks at 1636, 1380, and 1117 cm−1 in the CQD spectra are attributed to the CO and CC stretching vibrations, COO− symmetric stretching vibrations, and C–O tensile vibrations, respectively [43]. In addition, the FTIR spectra of the MOF and CQD@MOF consist of major absorption peaks at 668 and 768, 1407, and 1500-1600 cm−1, corresponding to the Zr–O, O–CO, and C–O–Zr vibration bands, respectively [44]. The additional broad absorption bands at 3600-3000 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of the CQDs were observed in the spectra of the CQD@MOF, which confirm the presence of the CQDs. The functional groups and chemical bonds of the pristine MOF, along with the functional groups of the CQDs, further validated the presence of the CQDs in the CQD@MOF nanohybrid [43]. As shown in Fig. 3e, the broad peak in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CQDs indicates the amorphous nature of the carbonaceous material [44]. The diffraction peaks at 5.6° (111), 9.18° (002), 10.66 (110), 11.33° (113), and 17.2° were indexed to the typical crystalline structure of CQD@MOF, which were similar to the characteristic peaks of the crystalline MOF [43,46]. Furthermore, small new peaks at 2θ = 25-30° indicate that the incorporation of CQDs in the CQD@MOF nanoflowers affected the crystal plane phase [47]. The changes in the pore size, pore diameter, and specific surface area after the incorporation of CQD were investigated by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis. Fig. 3f shows the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore-size distributions of the pristine MOF and CQD@MOF. The details are provided in Table S1. The average pore diameter in the CQD@MOF decreased from 4.1 to 3.6 nm and the pore volume decreased from 1.37 to 1.11 cm3g-1. Moreover, the corresponding specific area decreased from 1079.96 to 289.28 m2g-1. The MOF and CQD@MOF exhibited a similar N2 sorption isotherm (type IV), indicating the mesoporous nature of the prepared MOF and CQD@MOF, as shown in Fig. S4b [47]. Compared to the CQD@MOF, the pristine MOF exhibited larger absorbed volumes, indicating its larger pore volume. After incorporating CQDs into the MOF, the specific surface area and pore volume of CQD@ MOF decreased, because the CQDs occupied the MOF mesopores [48]. The CQD@MOF nanohybrid was synthesized with an increased particle size and abundant V0. MOFs have porous structures with well-defined pores and channels. CQDs can be encapsulated or hosted within these pores, leading to host-guest synergetic interaction [43,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic water-splitting performance of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrocatalytic performance of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode toward the HER and OER was evaluated in a 1 M KOH alkaline electrolyte solution using a three-electrode system consisting of Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, Pt as the counter electrode, and CQD@MOF/NF (1 cm2, excluding the area for contact) as the working electrode. The Ag/AgCl reference electrode was calibrated for LSV measurements at a scan rate of 0.5 mV/s in 1 M KOH electrolyte using the pre-purged system with highly pure Ar, as shown in Fig. S5. Considering E0 H2O/H2 = 0 V as the reference thermodynamic HER potential, the activity of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode toward the HER was significantly higher than that of NF, MOF/NF, and CQD/NF, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The onset overpotential of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode was 48 mV at 50 mA/cm2, which was significantly lower than that of NF (350 mV), MOF/NF (194 mV), and CQD/NF (151 mV). These results indicate a synergistic effect upon the hybridization of the CQD and MOF at the electrode interface [43]. Fig. 4b shows the corresponding Tafel plot, log|j| versus overpotential (ƞ), to verify the electrocatalytic kinetics of the HER, which indicates the potential required to achieve a 10-fold higher current density [48]. A lower Tafel slope value indicates the ability of the catalyst to achieve a higher current at a relatively lower overpotential [50]. The developed CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited the lowest slope value of 59 mV/dec, compared to the NF (117 mV/dec), MOF/NF (80 mV/dec), and CQD/NF (94 mV/dec) electrodes. These results confirm that the CQD@MOF/NF electrode required the lowest potential to achieve the desired current density. A low Tafel slope value indicates the high H atom adsorption and desorption of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode, in which the strong electron affinity of the central metal ion (Zr3/2) and the OH- functional groups on the CQDs can lead to the adsorption of H split from H2O on the MOF surface, amplifying water splitting and accelerating the HER [51]. The prepared CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited excellent OER performance, as shown in Fig. 4c, where the thermodynamic OER potential (E0 H2O/O2 = 1.23 V) was considered as the reference. To achieve a current density of 50 mA/cm2, the NF, MOF/NF, and CQD/NF electrodes exhibited OER potentials of 172, 142, and 138 mV, respectively, whereas the CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited an excellent OER activity of approximately 1.35 V at 50 mA/cm2. Notably, the enhanced oxygen evolution of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode was attributed to the strong intermolecular synergistic interaction between the CQD and MOF [44]. The hydrophilic nature of the MOF, and its porous micro-holes and zigzag flow channels facilitate the enhanced mass transfer of the electrolyte by preventing bubble overgrowth during oxygen evolution [48,51]. The CQDs in the zigzag channels of the MOF matrix accelerate mass transfer by decreasing the band-gap energy of the pristine MOF [45]. These excellent electrocatalytic OER kinetics were determined using the Tafel slope (Fig. 4d), as calculated using the following linear equation [51]:
(2)
ƞ = b l o g j + a
where, ƞ is the overpotential, j is the catalytic current density, b is the slope, and a is a constant. The Tafel slopes for the OER activity of the NF, MOF/NF, CQD/NF, and CQD@MOF/NF electrodes were determined to be 110, 84, 94, and 56 mV/dec, respectively. The Tafel slope for the OER and HER activity of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode was lower than that of previously reported electrocatalysts. Notably, the prepared CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited a lower Tafel slope for both HER and OER, indicating their faster kinetics toward both processes [12]. In addition to the reaction kinetics, the linear behavior of the Tafel plots demonstrates the high efficiency and durability of the catalyst toward the OER [50]. This was ascribed to the improved V0, as confirmed by the XPS analyses, which allows the generation of more active defects for the OER and alters the surface electronic structure to enhance the electrocatalytic activity. In particular, the incorporation of CQDs improves V0 in the CQD@MOF nanohybrid [43,46,52,53]. Moreover, a higher mass transfer rate was presumed to realize high-performance water splitting owing to the negative shift in the band-gap energy of the CQD@MOF nanohybrid after the introduction of CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 4e and f [24,54]. The MOF exhibited a band-gap energy of 3.86 eV. In contrast, the band-gap energy of CQD@MOF decreased to 3.75 eV, which supports the positive effect of CQD incorporation into the MOF. A lower bandgap enhances electron transport because of the lower energy required to excite electrons from the valence band to the conduction band [54,55]. The incorporation of CQDs into the porous networks of MOF can lead to charge transfer interactions, which results in the shift in the energy level, thereby affecting the band gap [43,49]. The reduced pore size of CQD@MOF determined by analyzing BJH pore size distributions and the decreased band-gap energy extracted from the Tauc plot indicate the host-guest synergetic interactions between CQDs and MOF.

The electrocatalytic mechanism of water splitting includes the adsorption of the reactant on the active sites of the electrode, its conversion to a product with a series of intermediates in a stepwise manner, and finally, the desorption of the products, which are H2 and O2 with the by-products [17,18]. The mechanism underlying the HER and OER at the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode are illustrated in Fig. 5 a and b, respectively. The relatively smaller Tafel slope for the HER, suggests faster kinetics where the Volmer–Heyrovsky mechanism is applicable for the HER activity of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode in alkaline media [50]. The corresponding mechanism demonstrates that the rate-determining step involves the H2O discharge reaction (Volmer step), followed by the electrochemical atom-ion desorption of the hydrogen from the electrode surface (H–H+, Heyrovsky step) [51]:

where, * refers to the electroactive sites and H* refers to the adsorbed hydrogen atom on the active sites. First, the metal ions (Zr3/2 and Zr5/2) of the MOF are reduced to the metallic state, forming a metal cluster, which facilitates the adsorption of hydrogen atoms [45]. The Volmer step includes the breaking of the O–H bond by proton discharge and initial electron transfer [43]. The discharged proton takes up the electrons from the electrode catalyst surface and forms a H atom, which is adsorbed on the same surface to form a meta-hydride bond (Fig. 5a) [43]. In the Heyrovsky step, the adsorbed H atoms are desorbed from the surface by combining with the water molecules in an alkaline medium to form H2 gas molecules [9,51]. These steps involve the electrochemical reduction of the adsorbed H2O molecules into H2 gas [50,51]. Moreover, open metal sites with high valence states, V0, and surface areas owing to the CQDs in the MOF reduce the rate-determining energy barrier in the HER [10]. The OER mechanism involves the formation of a metal-oxide bond, followed by the formation of metal hydroxide (MOOH) intermediate and breaking of MOOH to form O2 gas molecules, as shown in Fig. 5b [56]:

where M is the electroactive metal surface cation and OH− is the hydroxyl ion from the water molecule. The increased V0 owing to CQD incorporation plays a crucial role in increasing the number of active defects, which alters the surface electronic structure of CQD@MOF to enhance the OER kinetics [47]. The electroactive central metal in the MOF favors the adsorption of the oxygenated intermediates of OH*, OOH*, and O* on the surface of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode and enabled faster OER kinetics [56]. Moreover, the functional groups of the CQDs, such as COOH and OH, act as electron donors to facilitate the OER process.

To further verify the catalytic kinetic characteristics of the prepared electrocatalysts, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl), electrochemically active surface area (ECSA), turnover frequency (TOF), and charge transfer resistance (Rct) were determined by cyclic voltammetry (CV) at varying scan rates and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), as shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6a–h shows the relationship between the scan rate and the corresponding anodic/cathodic current densities for the HER and OER kinetics of the NF, MOF/NF, CQD/NF, and CQD@MOF/NF electrodes, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6d and h, the peak current density of the CQD@MOF/NF significantly increased, indicating enhanced electrocatalytic activity. The Cdl plots for each catalyst were obtained from the slope of the current density (mA/cm2) as a function of the scan rate (V/s) using the following equation [57]:
(9)
Δ j =
(
J a
−
J c
)
2
where Ja and Jc are the anodic and cathodic current density, respectively. Fig. 6i and j shows higher Cdl values of 87.6 mF/cm2 for HER and 83.7 mF/cm2 for OER in the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode than those of bare NF (5.33 mF/cm2 for HER and 5.8 mF/cm2 for OER), MOF/NF (34.6 mF/cm2 for HER and 32.3 mF/cm2 for OER), and CQD/NF (12.5 mF/cm2 for HER and 22 mF/cm2 for OER), which revealed the increase in the number of accessible active sites of CQD@MOF/NF. The highest ECSA values on the basis of Cdl indicate that CQD@MOF/NF exhibits a larger electrochemical surface area, which enhances the OER performance [57]. These results suggest that an increase in the specific catalytic surface area resulted in the closely packed layer-growth of CQD@MOF on the NF backbone and positive synergistic effect of the MOF and CQD [57]. The corresponding Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 6k and l and are fitted with the equivalent circuit in Fig. S6 to quantify the critical kinetic parameter Rct, which is in good agreement with the improved intrinsic activity of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode [55]. Significantly lower Rct values of approximately 5 Ω for the HER and 19 Ω for the OER were obtained for the CQD@MOF/NF electrode, as compared to those of bare NF (13.8 Ω for the HER and 20 Ω for the OER), MOF/NF (12 Ω for the HER and 24.3 Ω for the OER), and CQD/NF (20 Ω for the HER and 67 Ω for the OER), indicating the improved conductivity and charge transport characteristics of the developed CQD@MOF/NF electrode [58]. Moreover, the CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited the relatively high roughness factor (Rf) and TOF compared to those of bare NF, MOF, and CQDs, as shown in Table S2 and S3. The TOF reflecting the instantaneous efficiency of a catalyst, is derived from the number of turnovers of the catalytic cycles with respect to time per active site at the lowest concentration of the catalyst [43,59]. The proposed CQD@MOF/NF exhibited a comparatively higher TOF values of 106 and 104.52 s−1 for HER and OER, respectively, than that of NF (64.2/66 s−1 for HER and OER), MOF/NF (89/79 s−1 for HER and OER), and CQD/NF (76/71 s−1 for HER and OER). The integration of MOF and CQD synergistically improved the electrochemical properties, including the HER and OER performance, in relation to the individual MOF and CQD. The possible explanation for this improvement can be summarized: i) the mutual collaboration of the active metal sites in the MOF, which affects the intraframework pathways owing to the incorporation of CQDs, ii) low activation energy barrier induced by the increased V0, which corresponds to enhanced electron affinity, and iii) the supporting networks with improved conductivity, which are exposed to the redox active metal sites, promote the HER and OER performance of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode. The central atom of MOF provides an efficient adsorption site for the intermediates during HER and OER, which initiates the reaction at a much faster rate [13]. For this reason, CQD@MOF nanohybrid with such advances can potentially improve the HER and OER performance by the following mechanisms. 1) Open metal sites with high valence states, V0, and surface areas through the CQD incorporation into the MOF can reduce the rate-determining energy barrier of the HER [13]. 2) The V0 increased by the CQD incorporation which plays a crucial role in increasing the number of active sites, alters its electronic structure to enhance the OER kinetics. 3) The functional groups of the CQDs, such as COOH and OH, act as electron donors to facilitate the OER process [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fabrication process of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To fully investigate the electrocatalytic properties of the newly designed nanohybrids, the effects of each factor on the OWS performance, including the material concentration, synthesis parameters, and electrode fabrication parameters, should be considered [60,61][,]. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the HER and OER activities of the CQDs obtained at different synthesis temperatures (100, 140, 180, and 220 °C) were analyzed by adding the CQDs into the MOF precursor. The critical temperature for obtaining CQDs with superior electrocatalytic properties was determined to be 180 °C. Consequently, the CQD@MOF/NF electrode (@180 °C) exhibited exceptionally low HER and OER potentials, which is consistent with the previously discussed FL emission results for the CQDs (Section 2.1). The larger size of CQD@180 °C than that of the CQDs obtained at other temperatures (100, 140, and 220 °C) was fixed in the linker group of H2bpdc, resulting in an improved V0, which conferred the adsorption capability to the CQD@MOF electrode [28]. Fig. 7c and d shows the catalytic effects of the deposition temperature (120, 140, and 160 °C) and time (3, 5, and 7 h), respectively, on the HER and OER activities of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode. Among all potential combinations, the electrode fabricated at 140 °C for 5 h exhibited the lowest HER and OER potentials. At low temperatures and times, the electrocatalyst on the NF surface was not maintained, which resulted in the removal of the film during the water-splitting process, as shown in Fig. S7a. At higher temperatures and durations (160 °C for 5 and 7 h), the electrocatalyst was damaged, which decreased the number of V0 and functional groups [29,62,]. Based on the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Figs. S7b–d), the thermal stability of the prepared CQD@MOF electrocatalyst was higher than that of the MOF. In particular, the initial bond breaking started at temperatures of 148 °C with a longer time duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of scan rate, and electrolyte pH and type on the catalytic activity of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the scan rate was analyzed using CV in the region of 0.07–0.8 V and at scan rates of 1–8 mV/s with a step size of 1 mV/s (Fig. 8 a). The current peaks increased with the scan rate up to 5 mV/s. At scan rates greater than 5 mV/s, the current density gradually decreased. Therefore, a scan rate of 5 mV/s was determined to be optimal for further HER and OER. HER and OER measurements were performed using LSV in three different electrolytes with varying pH values (0.5 M sulfuric acid (H2SO4) (pH = 0), 1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7), and 1 M KOH (pH = 14)) at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The resulting LSV polarization curves in Fig. 8b and c shows that the HER and OER increased with an increasing pH (from acidic to alkaline media). The required potential to achieve a current density of 100 mA/cm2 was 126 mV (0.5 M H2SO4), 336 mV (1 M PBS), and 44 mV (1 M KOH) for the HER and 141 mV (0.5 M H2SO4), 151 mV (1 M PBS), and 134 mV (1 M KOH) for the OER. These results indicate the function of the prepared CQD@MOF/NF electrode in acidic and alkaline media, and the relatively poor efficiency in the neutral state with inadequate OH− ions, which act as charge carriers in the electrochemical cell [57]. The best results for both HER and OER were obtained in an alkaline medium, 1 M KOH solution electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 8d and e, the LSV polarization curves for HER and OER in different molarities of the KOH solution electrolyte demonstrate the higher HER and OER catalytic activities of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode in a 1 M KOH solution. The improved performance can be explained by the increased mobility and reaction kinetics induced by the increased OH- ion transport rates in the 1 M KOH solution [57,62,64]. In contrast, highly acidic (0.5 M H2SO4) or alkaline (3 and 6 M KOH) electrolytes can damage the electrode surface, leading to slower reaction kinetics and hampered electroactivity [64,63]. HER and OER polarization curves of the developed CQD@MOF/NF in water and 1 M KOH, is shown in Fig. 8f and S8. The required potential for HER and OER in tap water was 324 mV and 164 mV at 50 mA/cm2, which were higher than that in 1 M KOH. It can be attributed to the presence of dissolved salts, heavy metallic ions, organic compounds, and interfering ions in tap water, which block the active sites on the electrode, thereby resulting in a slower catalytic reaction rate [64]. The addition of ionic solutions into the water, such as KOH, has been widely used to increase the ion concentration and improve the smooth ionic flow [65]. However, in this study, we aimed to demonstrate the ability of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF electrode which can function even in tap water and emphasize its practical and potential applications in industry. The higher potential of the proposed CQD@MOF/NF confirmed its ability to potentially split water without the addition of an ionic solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term stability and durability of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode
ce:section.ce:para: After 3000 CV cycles, the cathodic polarization curves for both the HER and OER were almost identical, and their initial shapes were maintained (Fig. 9 a and b). The proposed CQD@MOF as a bifunctional catalyst under a potential of 1.00 V for the HER and 1.25 V for the OER with a current density of 10 ± 2 mA/cm2 exhibited excellent long-term stability without significant changes after operation for 11 h (Fig. 9c and d). This stability mainly originates from the intrinsic fast and easy H+/OH− ion adsorption and desorption of the MOF owing to its three-dimensional mesh-like porous structure, which allowed the CQD@MOF to rapidly regain its original state [66]. Moreover, the presence of O–H groups indicates the enhanced chemical stability of the CQD@MOF with the incorporation of CQD into the MOF matrix, which significantly promoted the long-term stability [43]. Additionally, CQDs which facilitate easy electron transfer between the electrode and electrolyte, decrease the damage to the electrode surface [35]. Therefore, the MOF porous networks inherited high chemical stability from the CQDs, imparting the prepared CQD@MOF/NF with better stability than that of reported electrocatalysts [,61,66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: OWS performance of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode in a two-electrode configuration
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent bifunctional electrocatalytic activity toward both the OER and HER in alkaline media, a water electrolyzer with a two-electrode setup was constructed with porous CQD@MOF/NF as the anode and cathode in a 1 M KOH solution, as shown in Fig. 10 a and Video S1. Fig. 10b shows the water-splitting performance with the required potential at 1.36 V of the water electrolyzer constructed using the CQD@MOF/NF electrode to afford 10 mA/cm2, which is lower than that of the bare NF//bare NF couple (1.86 V) and is among the lowest cell potential values reported for non-noble metal-based bifunctional catalysts [57]. The corresponding Tafel slope (Fig. 10c) was as small as 76 mV dec−1. The OWS activity of the proposed electrode is comparable with that of previously reported bifunctional electrocatalysts for HER, OER, and OWS in literature, as summarized in Table 1 [67–75]. Notably, the prepared CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited excellent long-term stability and durability, as evaluated after 4000 cycles and chronopotentiometry at 10 mA/cm2 in a 1 M KOH solution continuously for 17 h (Fig. 10d and e). The effect of aging for up to 60 days on the surface activity of the porous CQD@MOF electrocatalyst was investigated, as shown in Fig. 10f and S9. The synthesized CQD@MOF electrocatalyst exhibited outstanding stability and durability to sustain its performance for up to 60 d. The amounts of O2 and H2 extracted from the H-type electrolytic apparatus using the CQD@MOF/NF electrode were determined using customized gas-collection equipment, as shown in Fig. S10. The electrolyzed water reaction container was separated by an ion-exchange membrane. Fig. 10g and h shows the changes in the meniscus of the measuring cylinder for H2 and O2 generation at intervals of 5 min (0, 5, 10, and 15 min), respectively. The V(H2)/V(O2) ratio was determined to be approximately 2:1 (Fig. 10i), which is consistent with the theoretically calculated value produced by water splitting, indicating a nearly 100 % Faradaic efficiency toward full water splitting. These results confirmed that the CQD@MOF/NF proposed in this study is a highly efficient electrocatalyst for water splitting.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2023.118527

ce:section.ce:section-title: CO2 reduction of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical capture and conversion of CO2 to transform greenhouse gases into useful fuels is a promising technique [76–79]. As shown in Fig. 11 a and b, the linear polarization curve of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited the lowest potential of 332 mV to afford 10 mA/cm2, as compared to bare NF (852 mV), MOF/NF (547 mV), and CQD (526 mV). The CQD@MOF/NF electrode exhibited a low Tafel slope value of 78 mV/dec, which is considerably smaller than that of bare NF (197 mv/dec), MOF/NF (161 mv/dec), and CQD/NF (141 mv/dec). The low Tafel slope value indicates that the CO2 reduction on CQD@MOF/NF includes a two-electron electrochemical reduction mechanism [81]. The process starts with electron transfer to CO2 resulting in a CO2* intermediate, which is adsorbed on the metal surface [83–87]. Subsequently, the adsorbed CO2* obtains two protons and another electron and produces CO and H2O [86]. The absorbed CO2* interacts with the protons produced by water decomposition to form a COOH* intermediate [86]. COOH* is further reduced to CO* by a proton−electron transfer process, followed by CO* desorption from the catalyst surface to produce CO, as shown in the following processes [81,86]:
(10)

(11)

(13)

→ C O
where * represents the active adsorption sites. The V0 in the sub-layers enhance CO2 adsorption, resulting in faster CO2 conversion into CO [43]. The porous nature of MOFs facilitates the diffusion of CO2 and other reactants to the catalytic sites, thereby improving mass transport and reaction kinetics [79]. CQDs in well-defined structure of MOF modulate the surroundings of the active site, thereby enhancing the activity and selectivity in CO2 reduction [80]. The combination of MOFs and CQDs reduces the energy barrier for CO2 reduction, which decreases the overpotential required to drive the reaction and improves the energy efficiency [79,80]. The Faradaic efficiency of the CO2 conversion was 91 %, which is higher than that of previously reported electrocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 11c and Table 2 [76–78, 81–89]. The long-term durability of the developed CQD@MOF/NF electrode was determined in 0.1 M NaOH at 0.637 V by continuously maintaining a current density of approximately 50 mA/cm2 for at least 12 h, as shown in Fig. 11d. These results indicate its exceptional bifunctional water-splitting ability (HER/OER) and high CO2 adsorption on the electrode surface for improved CO2 reduction efficiency at lower cell voltages.

An effective, low-cost, and environmentally friendly approach was adopted to design a binder-free bifunctional electrocatalyst with nanoflower-like structure by using an optimized hydrothermal method. Toward environmental safety, the CQDs were obtained from kiwi as a natural source, and the MOF was synthesized by replacing toxic DMF with DI water as a supporting green solvent for water-splitting applications. The CQD@MOF nanohybrid delivered excellent HER and OER activities in an alkaline electrolyte. Key parameters, such as time and temperature of electrode deposition and pH and molarity of the electrolyte, were optimized to achieve the CQD@MOF nanohybrid exhibiting outstanding electrocatalytic performance. The incorporation of CQDs into the MOF matrix functioned as an efficient charge transfer unit that significantly improved the electrocatalytic properties. The feasibility of the CQD@MOF/NF electrode, which exhibited outstanding stability and durability for practical applications, for OWS based on a two-electrode system and CO2 reduction in energy storage electronics was verified. This study confirms the potential of the non-toxic, affordable, and bifunctional CQD@MOF electrocatalyst for multipurpose applications in the field of green energy.

Chemicals and apparatus: Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4; 98 %), H2bpdc (95 %), benzoic acid (C6H5COOH; >99.5 %), PBS; 0.1 M, pH = 7, N,N-dimethylmethanamide (98 %), KOH, H2SO4, methyl alcohol (CH3OH), ethanol, and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using DI water. The NF sheet was purchased from Xiamen Tmax Battery Equipment Ltd.

The CQDs were synthesized and purified via a one-step hydrothermal method using kiwi as the natural source, as shown in Fig. 1a. Fresh kiwi fruit was peeled off and blended into a juice. The obtained 50 mL kiwi juice was then transformed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After the completion of the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and a dark brown semi-solid solution was obtained. The obtained semi-solid solid was then centrifuged at 20,000 rpm for 30 min. The collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm micro-pore filter to remove large particles. Finally, the resulting CQDs were passed through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da for 24 h to remove impurities. The ultrapure water was changed every 6 h. The resulting CQD solution was stored at 4 °C until use.

ZrCl4 (0.240 g), C6H5COOH (2.456 g, for pore-making), and HCl (0.36 g, to inhibit the hydrolysis of pure ZrCl4) were mixed in 10 mL DMF and 20 mL DI, followed by the addition of H2bpdc (0.114 g). The precursor solution was mixed by magnetically stirring at room temperature until the solution turned milky green. Next, 0.142 mM CQD solution was added into the MOF precursor solution and stirred until the color of the solution changed to light yellow. The CQDs were slowly dispersed, and the color of the solution changed to light yellow. Subsequently, the resulting solution was further magnetically stirred for 20 min, and then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 20 h. A solid was naturally obtained after cooling to room temperature. The solid was then washed with water and suspended in DMF at room temperature for 12 h, followed by filtration and suspension in CH3OH at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, the obtained solid was washed with ethanol and vacuum-dried at 80 °C for further use.

First, the NF sheet was cut into 1 × 1 cm pieces, and washed with HCl, ethanol, and DI water for 10 min each to clean the surface before use. Subsequently, 20 mg of as-synthesized CQD@MOF powder was added to 10 mL of DI water and stirred until completely dissolved. The clean activated NF was dried in air at 60 °C for 30 min and subsequently immersed in the CQD@MOF solution in a Teflon tube. The Teflon tube was maintained at 100, 140, 180, and 220 °C for 6 h in a hot oven for the homogeneous deposition of CQD@MOF crystals on NF to fabricate the CQD@MOF/NF electrode.

XRD (SmartLab, Rigaku, Japan) measurements were performed to analyze the crystallinity of the CQD, MOF, and CQD@MOF. FTIR spectroscopy measurements were conducted using an FTIR-ATR spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS10). XPS was performed using a K-Alpha+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). SEM (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan), TEM, and EDS (Jem 2100F, JEOL) were performed to examine the surface morphologies and elemental configurations of the synthesized materials. To investigate the pore size distribution, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm measurements were conducted using a micromeritics adsorption analyzer (3 Flex, USA). Photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a FL spectrometer (QuantaMaster™400, PTI). UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometry (Agilent Technologies) was carried out to determine the band-gap energy of the prepared materials. TGA (SDQ 600, TA instrument) was performed under a N2 flow.

All electrocatalytic measurements were performed using standard three-electrode and two-electrode systems for OWS on a potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm DropSens) controlled by the DropView 8400 software. The three-electrode measurement setup consisted of Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode, Pt wire as the counter electrode, and NF as the working electrode. The catalytic activity toward the HER, OER, and OWS was analyzed using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in a 1 M KOH solution. The potential (E) of the three-electrode measurement was converted to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) based on the following relation: E [V vs RHE] = E (Ag/AgCl) + 0.2171 + 0.059 × pH. The potential of three-electrode measurements was converted to RHE based on the reference electrode calibration as shown in Fig. S5, i.e., E [V vs RHE] = E (Ag/AgCl) + 1.04. LSV polarization curves were measured in a 1.0 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The scanning range for the HER, OER, and CO2 reduction were 0 to −0.8 V vs RHE, 1.2–1.8 V vs RHE, and 0 to −2.0 V vs RHE, respectively. Prior to the experiment, the electrochemical cell was purged with Ar gas for 30 min. To reduce the additional ohmic resistance, bubbles generated on the electrode surface upon the HER and OER during the measurement were dispersed by stirring the solution at 500 rpm. The electrolyte was a 0.1 M NaOH solution with Ar or CO2 purged for at least 30 min. The controlled potential electrolysis of CO2 was performed in a H-cell system with Nafion membrane in ambient air.

Bhavna Hedau: Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sang-Joon Park: Visualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Byeong-Cheol Kang: Visualization, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Tae-Jun Ha: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hierarchical carbon microflowers supported defect-rich Co3S4 nanoparticles: an efficient electrocatalyst for overall water splitting
